resources.string: Guide the user\'s eye to the most important elements first using size, color, and spacing.
Use high contrast for text to ensure readability and to make important elements stand out.
Choose fonts that are easy to read and limit the number of different typefaces used.
Use colors to evoke emotions and create a cohesive look and feel for your application.
Don\'t crowd your UI. Use negative space to let elements breathe and reduce cognitive load.
Keep UI elements and behaviors consistent throughout the app to reduce learning time.
Always provide feedback for user actions, such as button presses or form submissions.
Design elements so their function is obvious (e.g., buttons should look clickable).
Ensure your app is usable by everyone, including people with visual or motor impairments.
Give users the freedom to undo actions and navigate easily through the app.
Design your UI to prevent errors from happening in the first place whenever possible.
Minimize the user\'s memory load by making objects, actions, and options visible.
Provide shortcuts and accelerators for expert users while keeping it simple for novices.
Keep it simple. Dialogs should not contain information that is irrelevant or rarely needed.
Provide easy-to-search documentation that focuses on the user\'s task.
The time to acquire a target is a function of the distance to and size of the target.
The time it takes to make a decision increases with the number and complexity of choices.
The average person can only keep 7 (plus or minus 2) items in their working memory.
Users spend most of their time on other sites. They prefer your site to work the same way.
Our brains naturally group similar elements together to make sense of complex images.
Related items should be placed close together to show they are part of a group.
Elements that look similar are perceived as part of the same group or having the same function.
Eyes follow the smoothest path when looking at lines, regardless of how they were drawn.
The brain will fill in missing parts of a design or image to create a whole.
Elements that move in the same direction are perceived as more related than those that don\'t.
Users have a preconceived idea of how things work based on their past experiences.
Show only what is necessary at the moment to avoid overwhelming the user.
Small, subtle animations that provide feedback and make the app feel alive.
Design for the smallest screen first to ensure the core experience is solid.
Design is a process. Continuously test, gather feedback, and improve your app.
